title: Latino Director Fede Álvarez Talks Alien: Romulus' Record-Breaking Success and Creative Process
text: Uruguayan filmmaker Fede Álvarez has achieved significant success with "Alien: Romulus," which earned $108.2 million globally during its opening weekend. In this interview with The Latin Times, Álvarez discusses how he and screenwriter Rodo Sayagues approached revitalizing the sci-fi horror franchise. The director emphasized their commitment to authenticity, stating: "We wanted to bring back that visceral, edgy feel that made the first Alien movie so terrifying." The story was deliberately positioned chronologically between Ridley Scott's 1979 original and James Cameron's 1986 "Aliens." A significant creative choice involved prioritizing practical effects over digital imagery. According to Álvarez, "There's something about seeing a real creature on screen that CGI just can't replicate." This approach enhanced audience immersion and fear response. The film achieved an 86% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes. Álvarez highlighted the importance of casting lesser-known actors like Cailee Spaeny, Isabela Merced, and David Jonsson, explaining that younger performers' on-screen deaths create stronger emotional impact. Additionally, Álvarez addressed the diminishing recognition of directorial vision in modern franchise filmmaking, advocating for greater director acknowledgment in marketing materials.
